Torii and Worship Hall |location = {{ill|Aomori, Aomori|ja|青森市}}, Yasukata 2-7-18, Aomori Prefecture |Coordinates = {{Coord|40|49|36.1|N|140|44|32.8|E|scale:25000_region:JP-02_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神}} |Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = Unknown |Main Hall = Shinmei-zukuri |Alternate Names = |Main Festival = September 15 |Rituals = |Map = {{location map|Japan Aomori|lat_deg=40|lat_min=49|lat_sec=36.1|lon_deg=140|lon_min=44|lon_sec=32.8|label=Uto Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{Coord|40|49|36.1|N|140|44|32.8|E}}}} }} '''Uto Shrine''' (善知鳥神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Aomori, Aomori|ja|青森市}}, Yasukata, Aomori Prefecture. Its former shrine rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}. == Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神}}, known as deities of the sea and maritime safety: {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵島姫命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|多岐津姫命}}, and {{ill|Tagirihime-no-Mikoto|ja|多紀理姫命}}. Additional deities worshipped include {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto|ja|倉稲魂命}}, {{ill|Miyabime-no-Mikoto|ja|宮比命}}, {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|ja|猿田彦命}}, and {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|海津見大神}}. == Overview == According to legend, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyo|ja|允恭天皇}}, a courtier named {{ill|Uto Yasukata|ja|善知鳥安方}} was exiled to Sotogahama. Inspired by a divine dream of Takakura Myojin, he built a small shrine on the tidal flats and enshrined the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神}}. Upon Yasukata's death, an unfamiliar bird appeared near the shrine. The male bird called "Uto," while the female bird called "Yasukata." Locals revered these birds as Yasukata’s incarnations. However, when a hunter mistakenly shot the male bird, the fields were ravaged, and the hunter died mysteriously, believed to be a curse. The male bird was then respectfully buried. During the time of {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}}, the shrine had fallen into ruin but was rebuilt under his command during the {{ill|Daido era|ja|大同}}. The shrine was later patronized by the {{ill|Nanbu clan|ja|南部氏}} during their rule and continued to receive support from the {{ill|Tsugaru clan|ja|津軽氏}} after their rise in the Edo period. In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine was designated a {{ill|Gosha|ja|郷社}} in 1873 and elevated to a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} in 1876. In 1907, as a part of the [[Shrine Consolidation Policy]] it incorporated the local {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷神社}} in July and {{ill|Watatsumi Shrine|ja|海津見神社}} in September. In December 2020, the shrine began offering {{ill|ema|ja|絵馬}} in collaboration with the {{ill|Virtual YouTuber|ja|バーチャルYouTuber}} {{ill|Aomori Rinko|ja|青森りんこ}}.{{Cite web|title=Collaboration Ema Now Available: Uto Shrine × "Aomori Rinko"|url=https://www.navitabi.jp/article/3831|website=Navitabi North Tohoku|accessdate=2021-01-14}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Rhinoceros auklet|ja|ウトウ}} (Uto/sea bird) * {{ill|Utou|ja|善知鳥}} (Noh play) * {{ill|Shikō Munakata|ja|棟方志功}} – a printmaker and artist who spent his childhood near the shrine. == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat}} * [http://utojinja.sakura.ne.jp/ Official Website of Uto Shrine] * [http://www.aomori-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja/tousei/jinja/12.htm Uto Shrine - {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Utō Shrine | image = [[File:Cerorhinca_monocerata.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | coordinates = {{Coord|40|49|36.1|N|140|44|32.8|E|scale:25000_region:JP-02_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 2-7-18 Anzokata, Aomori City, Aomori Prefecture, Japan | deity = {{ill|Munakata Three Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神}} | type = Former [[Ken-sha|Prefectural Shrine]] | founded = Unknown | architecture = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | festivals = {{nihongo|September 15|9月15日}} | map = Japan Aomori }} '''Utō Shrine''' ({{nihongo|善知鳥神社|うとうじんじゃ}}) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Aomori City|ja|青森市}}, Aomori Prefecture, Japan. It was previously classified as a [[Ken-sha|Prefectural Shrine]] under the prewar [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. == Deities == The shrine primarily enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Three Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神}}—Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto, Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto, and Tagirihime-no-Mikoto—deities of the sea and maritime safety. Additionally, the shrine honors deities such as {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命}}, {{ill|Miyabime|ja|宮比命}}, {{ill|Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦命}}, and Kaijin ({{nihongo|Watatsumi Ōkami|海津見大神}}). == Overview == The origins of the shrine date back to the reign of [[Emperor Ingyō]] when a noble named {{nihongo|Utō Chūnagon Yasukata|善知鳥中納言安方}} was exiled to the outer shores ({{nihongo|Sotogahama|外ヶ浜}}). Inspired by a divine revelation from Takakura Myōjin, Yasukata constructed a small shrine on the tidal flats and enshrined the Munakata Three Goddesses. After his death, a strange bird appeared near the shrine; its male cried "Utō," and its female cried "Yasukata," leading locals to revere the bird as a manifestation of Yasukata. However, when a hunter mistakenly killed the male bird, misfortune followed, prompting the villagers to conduct proper rites for the bird. In later years, the shrine fell into disrepair. During the {{nihongo|Daidō era|大同}}, {{nihongo|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|坂上田村麻呂}} restored the shrine. From the {{nihongo|Kenin|建仁}} to {{nihongo|Tenshō|天正}} eras (1201–1592), the {{nihongo|Nanbu clan|南部氏}} supported the shrine, followed by the {{nihongo|Tsugaru clan|津軽氏}} during the Edo period. In the Meiji era, the shrine was elevated to {{nihongo|Gōsha|郷社}} in 1873 and to {{nihongo|Ken-sha|県社}} in 1876. In 1907, the shrine absorbed the nearby {{nihongo|Inari Shrine|稲荷神社}} and {{nihongo|Kaijin Shrine|海津見神社}}. In December 2020, the shrine collaborated with the [[Virtual YouTuber]] {{nihongo|Aomori Rinko|青森りんこ}} to offer custom-designed {{nihongo|Ema|絵馬}} (wooden prayer plaques).{{Cite web|title=コラボ絵馬 授与開始 善知鳥神社×「青森りんこ"|url=https://www.navitabi.jp/article/3831|website=なびたび北東北|accessdate=2021-01-14|language=ja}} == Related Topics == * {{ill|Rhinoceros auklet|ja|ウトウ}} (Utō bird) * The [[Noh]] play ''{{nihongo|Utō|善知鳥}}'' * {{ill|Shikō Munakata|ja|棟方志功}}, a notable printmaker and painter who spent his childhood near the shrine. == External Links == * [http://utojinja.sakura.ne.jp/ Utō Shrine Official Website] (in Japanese) * [http://www.aomori-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja/tousei/jinja/12.htm Utō Shrine] - Aomori Prefecture Shrine Association (in Japanese) * [https://utojinja.amebaownd.com/ Official Blog] (in Japanese) {{Shinto shrines}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Aomori Prefecture]] [[Category:Aomori City]] [[Category:Religious buildings in Aomori Prefecture]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Munakata Shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:Https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[ja:善知鳥神社]]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Utō Shrine | image = [[File:Cerorhinca_monocerata.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | coordinates = {{Coord|40|49|36.1|N|140|44|32.8|E|scale:25000_region:JP-02_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 2-7-18 Anzokata, Aomori City, Aomori Prefecture, Japan | deity = {{ill|Munakata Three Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神}} | type = Former [[Ken-sha|Prefectural Shrine]] | founded = Unknown | architecture = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | festivals = {{nihongo|September 15|9月15日}} | map = Japan Aomori }} '''Utō Shrine''' ({{nihongo|善知鳥神社|うとうじんじゃ}}) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Aomori City|ja|青森市}}, Aomori Prefecture, Japan. It was previously classified as a [[Ken-sha|Prefectural Shrine]] under the prewar [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. == Deities == The shrine primarily enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Three Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神}}—Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto, Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto, and Tagirihime-no-Mikoto—deities of the sea and maritime safety. Additionally, the shrine honors deities such as {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命}}, {{ill|Miyabime|ja|宮比命}}, {{ill|Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦命}}, and Kaijin ({{nihongo|Watatsumi Ōkami|海津見大神}}). == Overview == The origins of the shrine date back to the reign of [[Emperor Ingyō]] when a noble named {{nihongo|Utō Chūnagon Yasukata|善知鳥中納言安方}} was exiled to the outer shores ({{nihongo|Sotogahama|外ヶ浜}}). Inspired by a divine revelation from Takakura Myōjin, Yasukata constructed a small shrine on the tidal flats and enshrined the Munakata Three Goddesses. After his death, a strange bird appeared near the shrine; its male cried "Utō," and its female cried "Yasukata," leading locals to revere the bird as a manifestation of Yasukata. However, when a hunter mistakenly killed the male bird, misfortune followed, prompting the villagers to conduct proper rites for the bird. In later years, the shrine fell into disrepair. During the {{nihongo|Daidō era|大同}}, {{nihongo|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|坂上田村麻呂}} restored the shrine. From the {{nihongo|Kenin|建仁}} to {{nihongo|Tenshō|天正}} eras (1201–1592), the {{nihongo|Nanbu clan|南部氏}} supported the shrine, followed by the {{nihongo|Tsugaru clan|津軽氏}} during the Edo period. In the Meiji era, the shrine was elevated to {{nihongo|Gōsha|郷社}} in 1873 and to {{nihongo|Ken-sha|県社}} in 1876. In 1907, the shrine absorbed the nearby {{nihongo|Inari Shrine|稲荷神社}} and {{nihongo|Kaijin Shrine|海津見神社}}. In December 2020, the shrine collaborated with the [[Virtual YouTuber]] {{nihongo|Aomori Rinko|青森りんこ}} to offer custom-designed {{nihongo|Ema|絵馬}} (wooden prayer plaques).{{Cite web|title=コラボ絵馬 授与開始 善知鳥神社×「青森りんこ"|url=https://www.navitabi.jp/article/3831|website=なびたび北東北|accessdate=2021-01-14|language=ja}} == Related Topics == * {{ill|Rhinoceros auklet|ja|ウトウ}} (Utō bird) * The [[Noh]] play ''{{nihongo|Utō|善知鳥}}'' * {{ill|Shikō Munakata|ja|棟方志功}}, a notable printmaker and painter who spent his childhood near the shrine. == External Links == * [http://utojinja.sakura.ne.jp/ Utō Shrine Official Website] (in Japanese) * [http://www.aomori-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja/tousei/jinja/12.htm Utō Shrine] - Aomori Prefecture Shrine Association (in Japanese) * [https://utojinja.amebaownd.com/ Official Blog] (in Japanese) {{Shinto shrines}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Aomori Prefecture]] [[Category:Aomori City]] [[Category:Religious buildings in Aomori Prefecture]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Munakata Shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[ja:善知鳥神社]]

{{Cite sources|date=August 2020}} {{Shrine |Name = Uto Shrine |image = [[File:Cerorhinca_monocerata.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Worship Hall |location = {{ill|Aomori, Aomori|ja|青森市}}, Yasukata 2-7-18, Aomori Prefecture |Coordinates = {{Coord|40|49|36.1|N|140|44|32.8|E|scale:25000_region:JP-02_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神}} |Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = Unknown |Main Hall = Shinmei-zukuri |Alternate Names = |Main Festival = September 15 |Rituals = |Map = {{location map|Japan Aomori|lat_deg=40|lat_min=49|lat_sec=36.1|lon_deg=140|lon_min=44|lon_sec=32.8|label=Uto Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{Coord|40|49|36.1|N|140|44|32.8|E}}}} }} '''Uto Shrine''' (善知鳥神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Aomori, Aomori|ja|青森市}}, Yasukata, Aomori Prefecture. Its former shrine rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}. == Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神}}, known as deities of the sea and maritime safety: {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵島姫命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|多岐津姫命}}, and {{ill|Tagirihime-no-Mikoto|ja|多紀理姫命}}. Additional deities worshipped include {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto|ja|倉稲魂命}}, {{ill|Miyabime-no-Mikoto|ja|宮比命}}, {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|ja|猿田彦命}}, and {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|海津見大神}}. == Overview == According to legend, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyo|ja|允恭天皇}}, a courtier named {{ill|Uto Yasukata|ja|善知鳥安方}} was exiled to Sotogahama. Inspired by a divine dream of Takakura Myojin, he built a small shrine on the tidal flats and enshrined the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神}}. Upon Yasukata's death, an unfamiliar bird appeared near the shrine. The male bird called "Uto," while the female bird called "Yasukata." Locals revered these birds as Yasukata’s incarnations. However, when a hunter mistakenly shot the male bird, the fields were ravaged, and the hunter died mysteriously, believed to be a curse. The male bird was then respectfully buried. During the time of {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}}, the shrine had fallen into ruin but was rebuilt under his command during the {{ill|Daido era|ja|大同}}. The shrine was later patronized by the {{ill|Nanbu clan|ja|南部氏}} during their rule and continued to receive support from the {{ill|Tsugaru clan|ja|津軽氏}} after their rise in the Edo period. In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine was designated a {{ill|Gosha|ja|郷社}} in 1873 and elevated to a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} in 1876. In 1907, as a part of the [[Shrine Consolidation Policy]] it incorporated the local {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷神社}} in July and {{ill|Watatsumi Shrine|ja|海津見神社}} in September. In December 2020, the shrine began offering {{ill|ema|ja|絵馬}} in collaboration with the {{ill|Virtual YouTuber|ja|バーチャルYouTuber}} {{ill|Aomori Rinko|ja|青森りんこ}}.{{Cite web|title=Collaboration Ema Now Available: Uto Shrine × "Aomori Rinko"|url=https://www.navitabi.jp/article/3831|website=Navitabi North Tohoku|accessdate=2021-01-14}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Rhinoceros auklet|ja|ウトウ}} (Uto/sea bird) * {{ill|Utou|ja|善知鳥}} (Noh play) * {{ill|Shikō Munakata|ja|棟方志功}} – a printmaker and artist who spent his childhood near the shrine. == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat}} * [http://utojinja.sakura.ne.jp/ Official Website of Uto Shrine] * [http://www.aomori-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja/tousei/jinja/12.htm Uto Shrine - Aomori Prefecture Shrine Association] * [https://utojinja.amebaownd.com/ Official Blog: Guide to Worship] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[category:kk]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Shrines in Aomori Prefecture]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[category:kk]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Aomori Prefecture]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[category:kk]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:History of Aomori City]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[category:kk]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Buildings in Aomori City]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[category:kk]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[category:kk]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[category:kk]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[category:kk]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[category:kk]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:Utō Shrine]][[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[ja:善知鳥神社]]

During the {{nihongo|Daidō era|大同}}, {{nihongo|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|坂上田村麻呂}} restored the shrine. From the {{nihongo|Kenin|建仁}} to {{nihongo|Tenshō|天正}} eras (1201–1592), the {{nihongo|Nanbu clan|南部氏}} supported the shrine, followed by the {{nihongo|Tsugaru clan|津軽氏}} during the Edo period. In the Meiji era, the shrine was elevated to {{nihongo|Gōsha|郷社}} in 1873 and to {{nihongo|Ken-sha|県社}} in 1876. In 1907, the shrine absorbed the nearby {{nihongo|Inari Shrine|稲荷神社}} and {{nihongo|Kaijin Shrine|海津見神社}}. In December 2020, the shrine collaborated with the [[Virtual YouTuber]] {{nihongo|Aomori Rinko|青森りんこ}} to offer custom-designed {{nihongo|Ema|絵馬}} (wooden prayer plaques).{{Cite web|title=コラボ絵馬 授与開始 善知鳥神社×「青森りんこ"|url=https://www.navitabi.jp/article/3831|website=なびたび北東北|accessdate=2021-01-14|language=ja}} == Related Topics == * {{ill|Rhinoceros auklet|ja|ウトウ}} (Utō bird) * The [[Noh]] play ''{{nihongo|Utō|善知鳥}}'' * {{ill|Shikō Munakata|ja|棟方志功}}, a notable printmaker and painter who spent his childhood near the shrine. == External Links == * [http://utojinja.sakura.ne.jp/ Utō Shrine Official Website] (in Japanese) * [http://www.aomori-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja/tousei/jinja/12.htm Utō Shrine] - Aomori Prefecture Shrine Association (in Japanese) * [https://utojinja.amebaownd.com/ Official Blog] (in Japanese) {{Shinto shrines}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT::Utō Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Aomori Prefecture]] [[Category:Aomori City]] [[Category:Religious buildings in Aomori Prefecture]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:defautsort]] [[Category:fix the O]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Utou Shrine}}[[category:fix the O]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:defautsort]] [[Category:fix the O]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Utou Shrine}}[[category:fix the O]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:defautsort]] [[Category:fix the O]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Utou Shrine}}[[category:fix the O]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:defautsort]] [[Category:fix the O]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Utou Shrine}}[[category:fix the O]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Munakata Shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[ja:善知鳥神社]]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Utō Shrine | image = [[File:Cerorhinca_monocerata.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | coordinates = {{Coord|40|49|36.1|N|140|44|32.8|E|scale:25000_region:JP-02_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 2-7-18 Anzokata, Aomori City, Aomori Prefecture, Japan | deity = {{ill|Munakata Three Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神}} | type = Former [[Ken-sha|Prefectural Shrine]] | founded = Unknown | architecture = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | festivals = {{nihongo|September 15|9月15日}} | map = Japan Aomori }} '''Utō Shrine''' ({{nihongo|善知鳥神社|うとうじんじゃ}}) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Aomori City|ja|青森市}}, Aomori Prefecture, Japan. It was previously classified as a [[Ken-sha|Prefectural Shrine]] under the prewar [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. == Deities == The shrine primarily enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Three Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神}}—Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto, Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto, and Tagirihime-no-Mikoto—deities of the sea and maritime safety. Additionally, the shrine honors deities such as {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命}}, {{ill|Miyabime|ja|宮比命}}, {{ill|Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦命}}, and Kaijin ({{nihongo|Watatsumi Ōkami|海津見大神}}). == Overview == The origins of the shrine date back to the reign of [[Emperor Ingyō]] when a noble named {{nihongo|Utō Chūnagon Yasukata|善知鳥中納言安方}} was exiled to the outer shores ({{nihongo|Sotogahama|外ヶ浜}}). Inspired by a divine revelation from Takakura Myōjin, Yasukata constructed a small shrine on the tidal flats and enshrined the Munakata Three Goddesses. After his death, a strange bird appeared near the shrine; its male cried "Utō," and its female cried "Yasukata," leading locals to revere the bird as a manifestation of Yasukata. However, when a hunter mistakenly killed the male bird, misfortune followed, prompting the villagers to conduct proper rites for the bird. In later years, the shrine fell into disrepair. During the {{nihongo|Daidō era|大同}}, {{nihongo|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|坂上田村麻呂}} restored the shrine. From the {{nihongo|Kenin|建仁}} to {{nihongo|Tenshō|天正}} eras (1201–1592), the {{nihongo|Nanbu clan|南部氏}} supported the shrine, followed by the {{nihongo|Tsugaru clan|津軽氏}} during the Edo period. In the Meiji era, the shrine was elevated to {{nihongo|Gōsha|郷社}} in 1873 and to {{nihongo|Ken-sha|県社}} in 1876. In 1907, the shrine absorbed the nearby {{nihongo|Inari Shrine|稲荷神社}} and {{nihongo|Kaijin Shrine|海津見神社}}. In December 2020, the shrine collaborated with the [[Virtual YouTuber]] {{nihongo|Aomori Rinko|青森りんこ}} to offer custom-designed {{nihongo|Ema|絵馬}} (wooden prayer plaques).{{Cite web|title=コラボ絵馬 授与開始 善知鳥神社×「青森りんこ"|url=https://www.navitabi.jp/article/3831|website=なびたび北東北|accessdate=2021-01-14|language=ja}} == Related Topics == * {{ill|Rhinoceros auklet|ja|ウトウ}} (Utō bird) * The [[Noh]] play ''{{nihongo|Utō|善知鳥}}'' * {{ill|Shikō Munakata|ja|棟方志功}}, a notable printmaker and painter who spent his childhood near the shrine. == External Links == * [http://utojinja.sakura.ne.jp/ Utō Shrine Official Website] (in Japanese) * [http://www.aomori-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja/tousei/jinja/12.htm Utō Shrine] - Aomori Prefecture Shrine Association (in Japanese) * [https://utojinja.amebaownd.com/ Official Blog] (in Japanese) {{Shinto shrines}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT::Utō Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Aomori Prefecture]] [[Category:Aomori City]] [[Category:Religious buildings in Aomori Prefecture]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:defautsort]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utou Shrine}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:defautsort]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utou Shrine}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:defautsort]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utou Shrine}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:defautsort]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utou Shrine}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Munakata Shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[ja:善知鳥神社]]

{{Cite sources|date=August 2020}} {{Shrine |Name = Uto Shrine |image = [[File:Cerorhinca_monocerata.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Worship Hall |location = {{ill|Aomori, Aomori|ja|青森市}}, Yasukata 2-7-18, Aomori Prefecture |Coordinates = {{Coord|40|49|36.1|N|140|44|32.8|E|scale:25000_region:JP-02_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神}} |Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = Unknown |Main Hall = Shinmei-zukuri |Alternate Names = |Main Festival = September 15 |Rituals = |Map = {{location map|Japan Aomori|lat_deg=40|lat_min=49|lat_sec=36.1|lon_deg=140|lon_min=44|lon_sec=32.8|label=Uto Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{Coord|40|49|36.1|N|140|44|32.8|E}}}} }} '''Uto Shrine''' (善知鳥神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Aomori, Aomori|ja|青森市}}, Yasukata, Aomori Prefecture. Its former shrine rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}. == Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神}}, known as deities of the sea and maritime safety: {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵島姫命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|多岐津姫命}}, and {{ill|Tagirihime-no-Mikoto|ja|多紀理姫命}}. Additional deities worshipped include {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto|ja|倉稲魂命}}, {{ill|Miyabime-no-Mikoto|ja|宮比命}}, {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|ja|猿田彦命}}, and {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|海津見大神}}. == Overview == According to legend, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyo|ja|允恭天皇}}, a courtier named {{ill|Uto Yasukata|ja|善知鳥安方}} was exiled to Sotogahama. Inspired by a divine dream of Takakura Myojin, he built a small shrine on the tidal flats and enshrined the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神}}. Upon Yasukata's death, an unfamiliar bird appeared near the shrine. The male bird called "Uto," while the female bird called "Yasukata." Locals revered these birds as Yasukata’s incarnations. However, when a hunter mistakenly shot the male bird, the fields were ravaged, and the hunter died mysteriously, believed to be a curse. The male bird was then respectfully buried. During the time of {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}}, the shrine had fallen into ruin but was rebuilt under his command during the {{ill|Daido era|ja|大同}}. The shrine was later patronized by the {{ill|Nanbu clan|ja|南部氏}} during their rule and continued to receive support from the {{ill|Tsugaru clan|ja|津軽氏}} after their rise in the Edo period. In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine was designated a {{ill|Gosha|ja|郷社}} in 1873 and elevated to a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} in 1876. In 1907, as a part of the [[Shrine Consolidation Policy]] it incorporated the local {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷神社}} in July and {{ill|Watatsumi Shrine|ja|海津見神社}} in September. In December 2020, the shrine began offering {{ill|ema|ja|絵馬}} in collaboration with the {{ill|Virtual YouTuber|ja|バーチャルYouTuber}} {{ill|Aomori Rinko|ja|青森りんこ}}.{{Cite web|title=Collaboration Ema Now Available: Uto Shrine × "Aomori Rinko"|url=https://www.navitabi.jp/article/3831|website=Navitabi North Tohoku|accessdate=2021-01-14}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Rhinoceros auklet|ja|ウトウ}} (Uto/sea bird) * {{ill|Utou|ja|善知鳥}} (Noh play) * {{ill|Shikō Munakata|ja|棟方志功}} – a printmaker and artist who spent his childhood near the shrine. == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat}} * [http://utojinja.sakura.ne.jp/ Official Website of Uto Shrine] * [http://www.aomori-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja/tousei/jinja/12.htm Uto Shrine - Aomori Prefecture Shrine Association] * [https://utojinja.amebaownd.com/ Official Blog: Guide to Worship] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[Category:Shrines in Aomori Prefecture]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Aomori Prefecture]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[Category:History of Aomori City]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[Category:Buildings in Aomori City]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:Utō Shrine]][[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[ja:善知鳥神社]]

revision.text:
{{Cite sources|date=August 2020}} {{Shrine |Name = Uto Shrine |image = [[File:Cerorhinca_monocerata.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Worship Hall |location = {{ill|Aomori, Aomori|ja|青森市}}, Yasukata 2-7-18, Aomori Prefecture |Coordinates = {{Coord|40|49|36.1|N|140|44|32.8|E|scale:25000_region:JP-02_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神}} |Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = Unknown |Main Hall = Shinmei-zukuri |Alternate Names = |Main Festival = September 15 |Rituals = |Map = {{location map|Japan Aomori|lat_deg=40|lat_min=49|lat_sec=36.1|lon_deg=140|lon_min=44|lon_sec=32.8|label=Uto Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{Coord|40|49|36.1|N|140|44|32.8|E}}}} }} '''Uto Shrine''' (善知鳥神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Aomori, Aomori|ja|青森市}}, Yasukata, Aomori Prefecture. Its former shrine rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}. == Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神}}, known as deities of the sea and maritime safety: {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵島姫命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|多岐津姫命}}, and {{ill|Tagirihime-no-Mikoto|ja|多紀理姫命}}. Additional deities worshipped include {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto|ja|倉稲魂命}}, {{ill|Miyabime-no-Mikoto|ja|宮比命}}, {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|ja|猿田彦命}}, and {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|海津見大神}}. == Overview == According to legend, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyo|ja|允恭天皇}}, a courtier named {{ill|Uto Yasukata|ja|善知鳥安方}} was exiled to Sotogahama. Inspired by a divine dream of Takakura Myojin, he built a small shrine on the tidal flats and enshrined the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神}}. Upon Yasukata's death, an unfamiliar bird appeared near the shrine. The male bird called "Uto," while the female bird called "Yasukata." Locals revered these birds as Yasukata’s incarnations. However, when a hunter mistakenly shot the male bird, the fields were ravaged, and the hunter died mysteriously, believed to be a curse. The male bird was then respectfully buried. During the time of {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}}, the shrine had fallen into ruin but was rebuilt under his command during the {{ill|Daido era|ja|大同}}. The shrine was later patronized by the {{ill|Nanbu clan|ja|南部氏}} during their rule and continued to receive support from the {{ill|Tsugaru clan|ja|津軽氏}} after their rise in the Edo period. In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine was designated a {{ill|Gosha|ja|郷社}} in 1873 and elevated to a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} in 1876. In 1907, as a part of the [[Shrine Consolidation Policy]] it incorporated the local {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷神社}} in July and {{ill|Watatsumi Shrine|ja|海津見神社}} in September. In December 2020, the shrine began offering {{ill|ema|ja|絵馬}} in collaboration with the {{ill|Virtual YouTuber|ja|バーチャルYouTuber}} {{ill|Aomori Rinko|ja|青森りんこ}}.{{Cite web|title=Collaboration Ema Now Available: Uto Shrine × "Aomori Rinko"|url=https://www.navitabi.jp/article/3831|website=Navitabi North Tohoku|accessdate=2021-01-14}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Rhinoceros auklet|ja|ウトウ}} (Uto/sea bird) * {{ill|Utou|ja|善知鳥}} (Noh play) * {{ill|Shikō Munakata|ja|棟方志功}} – a printmaker and artist who spent his childhood near the shrine. == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat}} * [http://utojinja.sakura.ne.jp/ Official Website of Uto Shrine] * [http://www.aomori-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja/tousei/jinja/12.htm Uto Shrine - Aomori Prefecture Shrine Association] * [https://utojinja.amebaownd.com/ Official Blog: Guide to Worship] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[Category:Shrines in Aomori Prefecture]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Aomori Prefecture]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[Category:History of Aomori City]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[Category:Buildings in Aomori City]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:Utō Shrine]][[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[ja:善知鳥神社]]

Torii and Worship Hall |location = {{ill|Aomori, Aomori|ja|青森市}}, Yasukata 2-7-18, Aomori Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|40|49|36.1|N|140|44|32.8|E|scale:25000_region:JP-02_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神}} |Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = Unknown |Main Hall = Shinmei-zukuri |Alternate names = |Main Festival = September 15 |Rituals = |Map = {{location map|Japan Aomori|lat_deg=40|lat_min=49|lat_sec=36.1|lon_deg=140|lon_min=44|lon_sec=32.8|label=Uto Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{Coord|40|49|36.1|N|140|44|32.8|E}}}} }} '''Uto Shrine''' (善知鳥神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Aomori, Aomori|ja|青森市}}, Yasukata, Aomori Prefecture. Additional deities worshipped include {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto|ja|倉稲魂命}}, {{ill|Miyabime-no-Mikoto|ja|宮比命}}, {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|ja|猿田彦命}}, and {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|海津見大神}}. == Overview == According to legend, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyo|ja|允恭天皇}}, a courtier named {{ill|Uto Yasukata|ja|善知鳥安方}} was exiled to Sotogahama. Inspired by a divine dream of Takakura Myojin, he built a small shrine on the tidal flats and enshrined the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神}}. Upon Yasukata's death, an unfamiliar bird appeared near the shrine. The male bird called "Uto," while the female bird called "Yasukata." Locals revered these birds as Yasukata’s incarnations. However, when a hunter mistakenly shot the male bird, the fields were ravaged, and the hunter died mysteriously, believed to be a curse. The male bird was then respectfully buried. During the time of {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}}, the shrine had fallen into ruin but was rebuilt under his command during the {{ill|Daido era|ja|大同}}. The shrine was later patronized by the {{ill|Nanbu clan|ja|南部氏}} during their rule and continued to receive support from the {{ill|Tsugaru clan|ja|津軽氏}} after their rise in the Edo period. In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine was designated a {{ill|Gosha|ja|郷社}} in 1873 and elevated to a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} in 1876. In 1907, as a part of the [[Shrine Consolidation Policy]] it incorporated the local {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷神社}} in July and {{ill|Watatsumi Shrine|ja|海津見神社}} in September. In December 2020, the shrine began offering {{ill|ema|ja|絵馬}} in collaboration with the {{ill|Virtual YouTuber|ja|バーチャルYouTuber}} {{ill|Aomori Rinko|ja|青森りんこ}}.{{Cite web|title=Collaboration Ema Now Available: Uto Shrine × "Aomori Rinko"|url=https://www.navitabi.jp/article/3831|website=Navitabi North Tohoku|accessdate=2021-01-14}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Rhinoceros auklet|ja|ウトウ}} (Uto/sea bird) * {{ill|Utou|ja|善知鳥}} (Noh play) * {{ill|Shikō Munakata|ja|棟方志功}} – a printmaker and artist who spent his childhood near the shrine. == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat}} * [http://utojinja.sakura.ne.jp/ Official Website of Uto Shrine] * [http://www.aomori-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja/tousei/jinja/12.htm Uto Shrine - Aomori Prefecture Shrine Association] * [https://utojinja.amebaownd.com/ Official Blog: Guide to Worship] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[Category:Shrines in Aomori Prefecture]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Aomori Prefecture]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[Category:History of Aomori City]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[Category:Buildings in Aomori City]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Shrine}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:Utō Shrine]][[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[ja:善知鳥神社]]

{{Cite sources|date=August 2020}} {{Shrine |name = Uto Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Cerorhinca_monocerata.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Worship Hall |location = {{ill|Aomori, Aomori|ja|青森市}}, Yasukata 2-7-18, Aomori Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|40|49|36.1|N|140|44|32.8|E|scale:25000_region:JP-02_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神}} |Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = Unknown |Main Hall = Shinmei-zukuri |Alternate names = |Main Festival = September 15 |Rituals = |Map = {{location map|Japan Aomori|lat_deg=40|lat_min=49|lat_sec=36.1|lon_deg=140|lon_min=44|lon_sec=32.8|label=Uto Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{Coord|40|49|36.1|N|140|44|32.8|E}}}} }} '''Uto Shrine''' (善知鳥神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Aomori, Aomori|ja|青森市}}, Yasukata, Aomori Prefecture. Its former shrine rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}. == Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神}}, known as deities of the sea and maritime safety: {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵島姫命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|多岐津姫命}}, and {{ill|Tagirihime-no-Mikoto|ja|多紀理姫命}}. Additional deities worshipped include {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto|ja|倉稲魂命}}, {{ill|Miyabime-no-Mikoto|ja|宮比命}}, {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|ja|猿田彦命}}, and {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|海津見大神}}. == Overview == According to legend, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyo|ja|允恭天皇}}, a courtier named {{ill|Uto Yasukata|ja|善知鳥安方}} was exiled to Sotogahama. Inspired by a divine dream of Takakura Myojin, he built a small shrine on the tidal flats and enshrined the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神}}. Upon Yasukata's death, an unfamiliar bird appeared near the shrine. The male bird called "Uto," while the female bird called "Yasukata." Locals revered these birds as Yasukata’s incarnations. However, when a hunter mistakenly shot the male bird, the fields were ravaged, and the hunter died mysteriously, believed to be a curse. The male bird was then respectfully buried. During the time of {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}}, the shrine had fallen into ruin but was rebuilt under his command during the {{ill|Daido era|ja|大同}}. The shrine was later patronized by the {{ill|Nanbu clan|ja|南部氏}} during their rule and continued to receive support from the {{ill|Tsugaru clan|ja|津軽氏}} after their rise in the Edo period. In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine was designated a {{ill|Gosha|ja|郷社}} in 1873 and elevated to a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} in 1876. In 1907, as a part of the [[Shrine Consolidation Policy]] it incorporated the local {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷神社}} in July and {{ill|Watatsumi Shrine|ja|海津見神社}} in September. In December 2020, the shrine began offering {{ill|ema|ja|絵馬}} in collaboration with the {{ill|Virtual YouTuber|ja|バーチャルYouTuber}} {{ill|Aomori Rinko|ja|青森りんこ}}.{{Cite web|title=Collaboration Ema Now Available: Uto Shrine × "Aomori Rinko"|url=https://www.navitabi.jp/article/3831|website=Navitabi North Tohoku|accessdate=2021-01-14}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Rhinoceros auklet|ja|ウトウ}} (Uto/sea bird) * {{ill|Utou|ja|善知鳥}} (Noh play) * {{ill|Shikō Munakata|ja|棟方志功}} – a printmaker and artist who spent his childhood near the shrine. == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat}} * [http://utojinja.sakura.ne.jp/ Official Website of Uto Shrine] * [http://www.aomori-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja/tousei/jinja/12.htm Uto Shrine - Aomori Prefecture Shrine Association] * [https://utojinja.amebaownd.com/ Official Blog: Guide to Worship] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Utou Shrine]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Utō Torii and Worship Hall |location = {{ill|Aomori, Aomori|ja|青森市}}, Yasukata 2-7-18, Aomori Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|40|49|36.1|N|140|44|32.8|E|scale:25000_region:JP-02_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神}} |Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = Unknown |Main Hall = Shinmei-zukuri |Alternate names = |Main Festival = September 15 |Rituals = |Map = {{location map|Japan Aomori|lat_deg=40|lat_min=49|lat_sec=36.1|lon_deg=140|lon_min=44|lon_sec=32.8|label=Uto Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{Coord|40|49|36.1|N|140|44|32.8|E}}}} }} '''Uto Shrine''' (善知鳥神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Aomori, Aomori|ja|青森市}}, Yasukata, Aomori Prefecture. Its former shrine rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}. == Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神}}, known as deities of the sea and maritime safety: {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵島姫命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|多岐津姫命}}, and {{ill|Tagirihime-no-Mikoto|ja|多紀理姫命}}. Additional deities worshipped include {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto|ja|倉稲魂命}}, {{ill|Miyabime-no-Mikoto|ja|宮比命}}, {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|ja|猿田彦命}}, and {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|海津見大神}}. == Overview == According to legend, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyo|ja|允恭天皇}}, a courtier named {{ill|Uto Yasukata|ja|善知鳥安方}} was exiled to Sotogahama. Inspired by a divine dream of Takakura Myojin, he built a small shrine on the tidal flats and enshrined the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神}}. Upon Yasukata's death, an unfamiliar bird appeared near the shrine. The male bird called "Uto," while the female bird called "Yasukata." Locals revered these birds as Yasukata’s incarnations. However, when a hunter mistakenly shot the male bird, the fields were ravaged, and the hunter died mysteriously, believed to be a curse. The male bird was then respectfully buried. During the time of {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}}, the shrine had fallen into ruin but was rebuilt under his command during the {{ill|Daido era|ja|大同}}. The shrine was later patronized by the {{ill|Nanbu clan|ja|南部氏}} during their rule and continued to receive support from the {{ill|Tsugaru clan|ja|津軽氏}} after their rise in the Edo period. In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine was designated a {{ill|Gosha|ja|郷社}} in 1873 and elevated to a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} in 1876. In 1907, as a part of the [[Shrine Consolidation Policy]] it incorporated the local {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷神社}} in July and {{ill|Watatsumi Shrine|ja|海津見神社}} in September. In December 2020, the shrine began offering {{ill|ema|ja|絵馬}} in collaboration with the {{ill|Virtual YouTuber|ja|バーチャルYouTuber}} {{ill|Aomori Rinko|ja|青森りんこ}}.{{Cite web|title=Collaboration Ema Now Available: Uto Shrine × "Aomori Rinko"|url=https://www.navitabi.jp/article/3831|website=Navitabi North Tohoku|accessdate=2021-01-14}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Rhinoceros auklet|ja|ウトウ}} (Uto/sea bird) * {{ill|Utou|ja|善知鳥}} (Noh play) * {{ill|Shikō Munakata|ja|棟方志功}} – a printmaker and artist who spent his childhood near the shrine. == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat}} * Torii and Worship Hall |location = {{ill|Aomori, Aomori|ja|青森市}}, Yasukata 2-7-18, Aomori Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|40|49|36.1|N|140|44|32.8|E|scale:25000_region:JP-02_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神}} |Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = Unknown |Main Hall = Shinmei-zukuri |Alternate names = |Main Festival = September 15 |Rituals = |Map = {{location map|Japan Aomori|lat_deg=40|lat_min=49|lat_sec=36.1|lon_deg=140|lon_min=44|lon_sec=32.8|label=Uto Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{Coord|40|49|36.1|N|140|44|32.8|E}}}} }} '''Uto Shrine''' (善知鳥神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Aomori, Aomori|ja|青森市}}, Yasukata, Aomori Prefecture. Its former shrine rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}. == Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神}}, known as deities of the sea and maritime safety: {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵島姫命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|多岐津姫命}}, and {{ill|Tagirihime-no-Mikoto|ja|多紀理姫命}}. Additional deities worshipped include {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto|ja|倉稲魂命}}, {{ill|Miyabime-no-Mikoto|ja|宮比命}}, {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|ja|猿田彦命}}, and {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|海津見大神}}. == Overview == According to legend, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyo|ja|允恭天皇}}, a courtier named {{ill|Uto Yasukata|ja|善知鳥安方}} was exiled to Sotogahama. Inspired by a divine dream of Takakura Myojin, he built a small shrine on the tidal flats and enshrined the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神}}. Upon Yasukata's death, an unfamiliar bird appeared near the shrine. The male bird called "Uto," while the female bird called "Yasukata." Locals revered these birds as Yasukata’s incarnations. However, when a hunter mistakenly shot the male bird, the fields were ravaged, and the hunter died mysteriously, believed to be a curse. The male bird was then respectfully buried. During the time of {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}}, the shrine had fallen into ruin but was rebuilt under his command during the {{ill|Daido era|ja|大同}}. The shrine was later patronized by the {{ill|Nanbu clan|ja|南部氏}} during their rule and continued to receive support from the {{ill|Tsugaru clan|ja|津軽氏}} after their rise in the Edo period. In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine was designated a {{ill|Gosha|ja|郷社}} in 1873 and elevated to a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} in 1876. In 1907, as a part of the [[Shrine Consolidation Policy]] it incorporated the local {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷神社}} in July and {{ill|Watatsumi Shrine|ja|海津見神社}} in September. In December 2020, the shrine began offering {{ill|ema|ja|絵馬}} in collaboration with the {{ill|Virtual YouTuber|ja|バーチャルYouTuber}} {{ill|Aomori Rinko|ja|青森りんこ}}.{{Cite web|title=Collaboration Ema Now Available: Uto Shrine × "Aomori Rinko"|url=https://www.navitabi.jp/article/3831|website=Navitabi North Tohoku|accessdate=2021-01-14}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Rhinoceros auklet|ja|ウトウ}} (Uto/sea bird) * {{ill|Utou|ja|善知鳥}} (Noh play) * {{ill|Shikō Munakata|ja|棟方志功}} – a printmaker and artist who spent his childhood near the shrine. == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat}} * Torii and Worship Hall |location = {{ill|Aomori, Aomori|ja|青森市}}, Yasukata 2-7-18, Aomori Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|40|49|36.1|N|140|44|32.8|E|scale:25000_region:JP-02_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神}} |Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = Unknown |Main Hall = Shinmei-zukuri |Alternate names = |Main Festival = September 15 |Rituals = |Map = {{location map|Japan Aomori|lat_deg=40|lat_min=49|lat_sec=36.1|lon_deg=140|lon_min=44|lon_sec=32.8|label=Uto Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{Coord|40|49|36.1|N|140|44|32.8|E}}}} }} '''Uto Shrine''' (善知鳥神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Aomori, Aomori|ja|青森市}}, Yasukata, Aomori Prefecture. Its former shrine rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}. == Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神}}, known as deities of the sea and maritime safety: {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵島姫命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|多岐津姫命}}, and {{ill|Tagirihime-no-Mikoto|ja|多紀理姫命}}. Additional deities worshipped include {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto|ja|倉稲魂命}}, {{ill|Miyabime-no-Mikoto|ja|宮比命}}, {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|ja|猿田彦命}}, and {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|海津見大神}}. == Overview == According to legend, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyo|ja|允恭天皇}}, a courtier named {{ill|Uto Yasukata|ja|善知鳥安方}} was exiled to Sotogahama. Inspired by a divine dream of Takakura Myojin, he built a small shrine on the tidal flats and enshrined the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神}}. Upon Yasukata's death, an unfamiliar bird appeared near the shrine. The male bird called "Uto," while the female bird called "Yasukata." Locals revered these birds as Yasukata’s incarnations. However, when a hunter mistakenly shot the male bird, the fields were ravaged, and the hunter died mysteriously, believed to be a curse. The male bird was then respectfully buried. During the time of {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}}, the shrine had fallen into ruin but was rebuilt under his command during the {{ill|Daido era|ja|大同}}. The shrine was later patronized by the {{ill|Nanbu clan|ja|南部氏}} during their rule and continued to receive support from the {{ill|Tsugaru clan|ja|津軽氏}} after their rise in the Edo period. In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine was designated a {{ill|Gosha|ja|郷社}} in 1873 and elevated to a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} in 1876. In 1907, as a part of the [[Shrine Consolidation Policy]] it incorporated the local {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷神社}} in July and {{ill|Watatsumi Shrine|ja|海津見神社}} in September. In December 2020, the shrine began offering {{ill|ema|ja|絵馬}} in collaboration with the {{ill|Virtual YouTuber|ja|バーチャルYouTuber}} {{ill|Aomori Rinko|ja|青森りんこ}}.{{Cite web|title=Collaboration Ema Now Available: Uto Shrine × "Aomori Rinko"|url=https://www.navitabi.jp/article/3831|website=Navitabi North Tohoku|accessdate=2021-01-14}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Rhinoceros auklet|ja|ウトウ}} (Uto/sea bird) * {{ill|Utou|ja|善知鳥}} (Noh play) * {{ill|Shikō Munakata|ja|棟方志功}} – a printmaker and artist who spent his childhood near the shrine. == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat}} * {{Cite sources|date=August 2020}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Uto Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Cerorhinca_monocerata.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Worship Hall |location = {{ill|Aomori, Aomori|ja|青森市}}, Yasukata 2-7-18, Aomori Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|40|49|36.1|N|140|44|32.8|E|scale:25000_region:JP-02_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神}} |Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = Unknown |Main Hall = Shinmei-zukuri |Alternate names = |Main Festival = September 15 |Rituals = |Map = {{location map|Japan Aomori|lat_deg=40|lat_min=49|lat_sec=36.1|lon_deg=140|lon_min=44|lon_sec=32.8|label=Uto Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{Coord|40|49|36.1|N|140|44|32.8|E}}}} }} '''Uto Shrine''' (善知鳥神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Aomori, Aomori|ja|青森市}}, Yasukata, Aomori Prefecture. Its former shrine rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}. == Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神}}, known as deities of the sea and maritime safety: {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵島姫命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|多岐津姫命}}, and {{ill|Tagirihime-no-Mikoto|ja|多紀理姫命}}. Additional deities worshipped include {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto|ja|倉稲魂命}}, {{ill|Miyabime-no-Mikoto|ja|宮比命}}, {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|ja|猿田彦命}}, and {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|海津見大神}}. == Overview == According to legend, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyo|ja|允恭天皇}}, a courtier named {{ill|Uto Yasukata|ja|善知鳥安方}} was exiled to Sotogahama. Inspired by a divine dream of Takakura Myojin, he built a small shrine on the tidal flats and enshrined the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神}}. Upon Yasukata's death, an unfamiliar bird appeared near the shrine. The male bird called "Uto," while the female bird called "Yasukata." Locals revered these birds as Yasukata’s incarnations. However, when a hunter mistakenly shot the male bird, the fields were ravaged, and the hunter died mysteriously, believed to be a curse. The male bird was then respectfully buried. During the time of {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}}, the shrine had fallen into ruin but was rebuilt under his command during the {{ill|Daido era|ja|大同}}. The shrine was later patronized by the {{ill|Nanbu clan|ja|南部氏}} during their rule and continued to receive support from the {{ill|Tsugaru clan|ja|津軽氏}} after their rise in the Edo period. In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine was designated a {{ill|Gosha|ja|郷社}} in 1873 and elevated to a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} in 1876. In 1907, as a part of the [[Shrine Consolidation Policy]] it incorporated the local {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷神社}} in July and {{ill|Watatsumi Shrine|ja|海津見神社}} in September. In December 2020, the shrine began offering {{ill|ema|ja|絵馬}} in collaboration with the {{ill|Virtual YouTuber|ja|バーチャルYouTuber}} {{ill|Aomori Rinko|ja|青森りんこ}}.{{Cite web|title=Collaboration Ema Now Available: Uto Shrine × "Aomori Rinko"|url=https://www.navitabi.jp/article/3831|website=Navitabi North Tohoku|accessdate=2021-01-14}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Rhinoceros auklet|ja|ウトウ}} (Uto/sea bird) * {{ill|Utou|ja|善知鳥}} (Noh play) * {{ill|Shikō Munakata|ja|棟方志功}} – a printmaker and artist who spent his childhood near the shrine. == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat}} * Torii and Worship Hall |location = {{ill|Aomori, Aomori|ja|青森市}}, Yasukata 2-7-18, Aomori Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|40|49|36.1|N|140|44|32.8|E|scale:25000_region:JP-02_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神}} |Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = Unknown |Main Hall = Shinmei-zukuri |Alternate names = |Main Festival = September 15 |Rituals = |Map = {{location map|Japan Aomori|lat_deg=40|lat_min=49|lat_sec=36.1|lon_deg=140|lon_min=44|lon_sec=32.8|label=Uto Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{Coord|40|49|36.1|N|140|44|32.8|E}}}} }} '''Uto Shrine''' (善知鳥神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Aomori, Aomori|ja|青森市}}, Yasukata, Aomori Prefecture. Its former shrine rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}. == Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神}}, known as deities of the sea and maritime safety: {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵島姫命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|多岐津姫命}}, and {{ill|Tagirihime-no-Mikoto|ja|多紀理姫命}}. Additional deities worshipped include {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto|ja|倉稲魂命}}, {{ill|Miyabime-no-Mikoto|ja|宮比命}}, {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|ja|猿田彦命}}, and {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|海津見大神}}. == Overview == According to legend, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyo|ja|允恭天皇}}, a courtier named {{ill|Uto Yasukata|ja|善知鳥安方}} was exiled to Sotogahama. Inspired by a divine dream of Takakura Myojin, he built a small shrine on the tidal flats and enshrined the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神}}. Upon Yasukata's death, an unfamiliar bird appeared near the shrine. The male bird called "Uto," while the female bird called "Yasukata." Locals revered these birds as Yasukata’s incarnations. However, when a hunter mistakenly shot the male bird, the fields were ravaged, and the hunter died mysteriously, believed to be a curse. The male bird was then respectfully buried. During the time of {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}}, the shrine had fallen into ruin but was rebuilt under his command during the {{ill|Daido era|ja|大同}}. The shrine was later patronized by the {{ill|Nanbu clan|ja|南部氏}} during their rule and continued to receive support from the {{ill|Tsugaru clan|ja|津軽氏}} after their rise in the Edo period. In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine was designated a {{ill|Gosha|ja|郷社}} in 1873 and elevated to a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} in 1876. In 1907, as a part of the [[Shrine Consolidation Policy]] it incorporated the local {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷神社}} in July and {{ill|Watatsumi Shrine|ja|海津見神社}} in September. In December 2020, the shrine began offering {{ill|ema|ja|絵馬}} in collaboration with the {{ill|Virtual YouTuber|ja|バーチャルYouTuber}} {{ill|Aomori Rinko|ja|青森りんこ}}.{{Cite web|title=Collaboration Ema Now Available: Uto Shrine × "Aomori Rinko"|url=https://www.navitabi.jp/article/3831|website=Navitabi North Tohoku|accessdate=2021-01-14}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Rhinoceros auklet|ja|ウトウ}} (Uto/sea bird) * {{ill|Utou|ja|善知鳥}} (Noh play) * {{ill|Shikō Munakata|ja|棟方志功}} – a printmaker and artist who spent his childhood near the shrine. == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat}} * [http://utojinja.sakura.ne.jp/ Official Website of Uto Shrine] * [http://www.aomori-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja/tousei/jinja/12.htm Uto Shrine - Aomori Prefecture Shrine Association] * [https://utojinja.amebaownd.com/ Official Blog: Guide to Worship] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Utou Shrine-qq]] {{Cite sources|date=August 2020}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Uto Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Cerorhinca_monocerata.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Worship Hall |location = {{ill|Aomori, Aomori|ja|青森市}}, Yasukata 2-7-18, Aomori Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|40|49|36.1|N|140|44|32.8|E|scale:25000_region:JP-02_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神}} |Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = Unknown |Main Hall = Shinmei-zukuri |Alternate names = |Main Festival = September 15 |Rituals = |Map = {{location map|Japan Aomori|lat_deg=40|lat_min=49|lat_sec=36.1|lon_deg=140|lon_min=44|lon_sec=32.8|label=Uto Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{Coord|40|49|36.1|N|140|44|32.8|E}}}} }} '''Uto Shrine''' (善知鳥神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Aomori, Aomori|ja|青森市}}, Yasukata, Aomori Prefecture. Its former shrine rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}. == Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神}}, known as deities of the sea and maritime safety: {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵島姫命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|多岐津姫命}}, and {{ill|Tagirihime-no-Mikoto|ja|多紀理姫命}}. Additional deities worshipped include {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto|ja|倉稲魂命}}, {{ill|Miyabime-no-Mikoto|ja|宮比命}}, {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|ja|猿田彦命}}, and {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|海津見大神}}. == Overview == According to legend, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyo|ja|允恭天皇}}, a courtier named {{ill|Uto Yasukata|ja|善知鳥安方}} was exiled to Sotogahama. Inspired by a divine dream of Takakura Myojin, he built a small shrine on the tidal flats and enshrined the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神}}. Upon Yasukata's death, an unfamiliar bird appeared near the shrine. The male bird called "Uto," while the female bird called "Yasukata." Locals revered these birds as Yasukata’s incarnations. However, when a hunter mistakenly shot the male bird, the fields were ravaged, and the hunter died mysteriously, believed to be a curse. The male bird was then respectfully buried. During the time of {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}}, the shrine had fallen into ruin but was rebuilt under his command during the {{ill|Daido era|ja|大同}}. The shrine was later patronized by the {{ill|Nanbu clan|ja|南部氏}} during their rule and continued to receive support from the {{ill|Tsugaru clan|ja|津軽氏}} after their rise in the Edo period. In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine was designated a {{ill|Gosha|ja|郷社}} in 1873 and elevated to a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} in 1876. In 1907, as a part of the [[Shrine Consolidation Policy]] it incorporated the local {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷神社}} in July and {{ill|Watatsumi Shrine|ja|海津見神社}} in September. In December 2020, the shrine began offering {{ill|ema|ja|絵馬}} in collaboration with the {{ill|Virtual YouTuber|ja|バーチャルYouTuber}} {{ill|Aomori Rinko|ja|青森りんこ}}.{{Cite web|title=Collaboration Ema Now Available: Uto Shrine × "Aomori Rinko"|url=https://www.navitabi.jp/article/3831|website=Navitabi North Tohoku|accessdate=2021-01-14}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Rhinoceros auklet|ja|ウトウ}} (Uto/sea bird) * {{ill|Utou|ja|善知鳥}} (Noh play) * {{ill|Shikō Munakata|ja|棟方志功}} – a printmaker and artist who spent his childhood near the shrine. == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat}} * [http://utojinja.sakura.ne.jp/ Official Website of Uto Shrine] * [http://www.aomori-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja/tousei/jinja/12.htm Uto Shrine - Aomori Prefecture Shrine Association] * {{Cite sources|date=August 2020}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Uto Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Cerorhinca_monocerata.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Worship Hall |location = {{ill|Aomori, Aomori|ja|青森市}}, Yasukata 2-7-18, Aomori Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|40|49|36.1|N|140|44|32.8|E|scale:25000_region:JP-02_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}} |Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = Unknown |Main Hall = Shinmei-zukuri |Alternate names = |Main Festival = September 15 |Rituals = |Map = {{location map|Japan Aomori|lat_deg=40|lat_min=49|lat_sec=36.1|lon_deg=140|lon_min=44|lon_sec=32.8|label=Uto Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{Coord|40|49|36.1|N|140|44|32.8|E}}}} }} '''Uto Shrine''' (善知鳥神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Aomori, Aomori|ja|青森市}}, Yasukata, Aomori Prefecture. Its former shrine rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}. == Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}, known as deities of the sea and maritime safety: {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵島姫命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|多岐津姫命}}, and {{ill|Tagirihime-no-Mikoto|ja|多紀理姫命}}. Additional deities worshipped include {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto|ja|倉稲魂命}}, {{ill|Miyabime-no-Mikoto|ja|宮比命}}, {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|ja|猿田彦命}}, and {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|海津見大神}}. == Overview == According to legend, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyo|ja|允恭天皇}}, a courtier named {{ill|Uto Yasukata|ja|善知鳥安方}} was exiled to Sotogahama. Inspired by a divine dream of Takakura Myojin, he built a small shrine on the tidal flats and enshrined the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}. Upon Yasukata's death, an unfamiliar bird appeared near the shrine. The male bird called "Uto," while the female bird called "Yasukata." Locals revered these birds as Yasukata’s incarnations. However, when a hunter mistakenly shot the male bird, the fields were ravaged, and the hunter died mysteriously, believed to be a curse. The male bird was then respectfully buried. During the time of {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}}, the shrine had fallen into ruin but was rebuilt under his command during the {{ill|Daido era|ja|大同}}. The shrine was later patronized by the {{ill|Nanbu clan|ja|南部氏}} during their rule and continued to receive support from the {{ill|Tsugaru clan|ja|津軽氏}} after their rise in the Edo period. In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine was designated a {{ill|Gosha|ja|郷社}} in 1873 and elevated to a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} in 1876. In 1907, as a part of the [[Shrine Consolidation Policy]] it incorporated the local {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷神社}} in July and {{ill|Watatsumi Shrine|ja|海津見神社}} in September. In December 2020, the shrine began offering {{ill|ema|ja|絵馬}} in collaboration with the {{ill|Virtual YouTuber|ja|バーチャルYouTuber}} {{ill|Aomori Rinko|ja|青森りんこ}}.{{Cite web|title=Collaboration Ema Now Available: Uto Shrine × "Aomori Rinko"|url=https://www.navitabi.jp/article/3831|website=Navitabi North Tohoku|accessdate=2021-01-14}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Rhinoceros auklet|ja|ウトウ}} (Uto/sea bird) * {{ill|Utou|ja|善知鳥}} (Noh play) * {{ill|Shikō Munakata|ja|棟方志功}} – a printmaker and artist who spent his childhood near the shrine. == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat}} * [http://utojinja.sakura.ne.jp/ Official Website of Uto Shrine] * [http://www.aomori-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja/tousei/jinja/12.htm Uto Shrine - Aomori Prefecture Shrine Association] * During the time of {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}}, the shrine had fallen into ruin but was rebuilt under his command during the {{ill|Daido era|ja|大同}}. The shrine was later patronized by the {{ill|Nanbu clan|ja|南部氏}} during their rule and continued to receive support from the {{ill|Tsugaru clan|ja|津軽氏}} after their rise in the Edo period. In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine was designated a {{ill|Gosha|ja|郷社}} in 1873 and elevated to a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} in 1876. In 1907, as a part of the [[Shrine Consolidation Policy]] it incorporated the local {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷神社}} in July and {{ill|Watatsumi Shrine|ja|海津見神社}} in September. In December 2020, the shrine began offering {{ill|ema|ja|絵馬}} in collaboration with the {{ill|Virtual YouTuber|ja|バーチャルYouTuber}} {{ill|Aomori Rinko|ja|青森りんこ}}.{{Cite web|title=Collaboration Ema Now Available: Uto Shrine × "Aomori Rinko"|url=https://www.navitabi.jp/article/3831|website=Navitabi North Tohoku|accessdate=2021-01-14}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Rhinoceros auklet|ja|ウトウ}} (Uto/sea bird) * {{ill|Utou|ja|善知鳥}} (Noh play) * {{ill|Shikō Munakata|ja|棟方志功}} – a printmaker and artist who spent his childhood near the shrine. == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat}} * [http://utojinja.sakura.ne.jp/ Official Website of Uto Shrine] * [http://www.aomori-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja/tousei/jinja/12.htm Uto Shrine - Aomori Prefecture Shrine Association] * {{Cite sources|date=August 2020}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Uto Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Cerorhinca_monocerata.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Worship Hall |location = {{ill|Aomori, Aomori|ja|青森市}}, Yasukata 2-7-18, Aomori Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|40|49|36.1|N|140|44|32.8|E|scale:25000_region:JP-02_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}} |Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = Unknown |Main Hall = Shinmei-zukuri |Alternate names = |Main Festival = September 15 |Rituals = |Map = {{location map|Japan Aomori|lat_deg=40|lat_min=49|lat_sec=36.1|lon_deg=140|lon_min=44|lon_sec=32.8|label=Uto Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{Coord|40|49|36.1|N|140|44|32.8|E}}}} }} '''Uto Shrine''' (善知鳥神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Aomori, Aomori|ja|青森市}}, Yasukata, Aomori Prefecture. Its former shrine rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}. == Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}, known as deities of the sea and maritime safety: {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵島姫命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|多岐津姫命}}, and {{ill|Tagirihime-no-Mikoto|ja|多紀理姫命}}. Additional deities worshipped include {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto|ja|倉稲魂命}}, {{ill|Miyabime-no-Mikoto|ja|宮比命}}, {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|ja|猿田彦命}}, and {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|海津見大神}}. == Overview == According to legend, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyo|ja|允恭天皇}}, a courtier named {{ill|Uto Yasukata|ja|善知鳥安方}} was exiled to Sotogahama. Inspired by a divine dream of Takakura Myojin, he built a small shrine on the tidal flats and enshrined the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}. Upon Yasukata's death, an unfamiliar bird appeared near the shrine. The male bird called "Uto," while the female bird called "Yasukata." Locals revered these birds as Yasukata’s incarnations. However, when a hunter mistakenly shot the male bird, the fields were ravaged, and the hunter died mysteriously, believed to be a curse. The male bird was then respectfully buried. During the time of {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}}, the shrine had fallen into ruin but was rebuilt under his command during the {{ill|Daido era|ja|大同}}. The shrine was later patronized by the {{ill|Nanbu clan|ja|南部氏}} during their rule and continued to receive support from the {{ill|Tsugaru clan|ja|津軽氏}} after their rise in the Edo period. In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine was designated a {{ill|Gosha|ja|郷社}} in 1873 and elevated to a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} in 1876. In 1907, as a part of the [[Shrine Consolidation Policy]] it incorporated the local {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷神社}} in July and {{ill|Watatsumi Shrine|ja|海津見神社}} in September. In December 2020, the shrine began offering {{ill|ema|ja|絵馬}} in collaboration with the {{ill|Virtual YouTuber|ja|バーチャルYouTuber}} {{ill|Aomori Rinko|ja|青森りんこ}}.{{Cite web|title=Collaboration Ema Now Available: Uto Shrine × "Aomori Rinko"|url=https://www.navitabi.jp/article/3831|website=Navitabi North Tohoku|accessdate=2021-01-14}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Rhinoceros auklet|ja|ウトウ}} (Uto/sea bird) * {{ill|Utou|ja|善知鳥}} (Noh play) * {{ill|Shikō Munakata|ja|棟方志功}} – a printmaker and artist who spent his childhood near the shrine. == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat}} * [http://utojinja.sakura.ne.jp/ Official Website of Uto Shrine] * [http://www.aomori-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja/tousei/jinja/12.htm Uto Shrine - Aomori Prefecture Shrine Association] * Its former shrine rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}. == Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}, known as deities of the sea and maritime safety: {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵島姫命|1=Ichikishimahime}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|多岐津姫命}}, and {{ill|Tagirihime-no-Mikoto|ja|多紀理姫命}}. Additional deities worshipped include {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto|ja|倉稲魂命}}, {{ill|Miyabime-no-Mikoto|ja|宮比命}}, {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|ja|猿田彦命}}, and {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|海津見大神}}. == Overview == According to legend, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyo|ja|允恭天皇}}, a courtier named {{ill|Uto Yasukata|ja|善知鳥安方}} was exiled to Sotogahama. Inspired by a divine dream of Takakura Myojin, he built a small shrine on the tidal flats and enshrined the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}. Upon Yasukata's death, an unfamiliar bird appeared near the shrine. The male bird called "Uto," while the female bird called "Yasukata." Locals revered these birds as Yasukata’s incarnations. However, when a hunter mistakenly shot the male bird, the fields were ravaged, and the hunter died mysteriously, believed to be a curse. The male bird was then respectfully buried. During the time of {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}}, the shrine had fallen into ruin but was rebuilt under his command during the {{ill|Daido era|ja|大同}}. The shrine was later patronized by the {{ill|Nanbu clan|ja|南部氏}} during their rule and continued to receive support from the {{ill|Tsugaru clan|ja|津軽氏}} after their rise in the Edo period. In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine was designated a {{ill|Gosha|ja|郷社}} in 1873 and elevated to a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} in 1876. In 1907, as a part of the [[Shrine Consolidation Policy]] it incorporated the local {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷神社}} in July and {{ill|Watatsumi Shrine|ja|海津見神社}} in September. In December 2020, the shrine began offering {{ill|ema|ja|絵馬}} in collaboration with the {{ill|Virtual YouTuber|ja|バーチャルYouTuber}} {{ill|Aomori Rinko|ja|青森りんこ}}.{{Cite web|title=Collaboration Ema Now Available: Uto Shrine × "Aomori Rinko"|url=https://www.navitabi.jp/article/3831|website=Navitabi North Tohoku|accessdate=2021-01-14}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Rhinoceros auklet|ja|ウトウ}} (Uto/sea bird) * {{ill|Utou|ja|善知鳥}} (Noh play) * {{ill|Shikō Munakata|ja|棟方志功}} – a printmaker and artist who spent his childhood near the shrine. == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat}} * Torii and Worship Hall |location = {{ill|Aomori, Aomori|ja|青森市}}, Yasukata 2-7-18, Aomori Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|40|49|36.1|N|140|44|32.8|E|scale:25000_region:JP-02_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}} |Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = Unknown |Main Hall = Shinmei-zukuri |Alternate names = |Main Festival = September 15 |Rituals = |Map = {{location map|Japan Aomori|lat_deg=40|lat_min=49|lat_sec=36.1|lon_deg=140|lon_min=44|lon_sec=32.8|label=Uto Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{Coord|40|49|36.1|N|140|44|32.8|E}}}} }} '''Uto Shrine''' (善知鳥神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Aomori, Aomori|ja|青森市}}, Yasukata, Aomori Prefecture. Its former shrine rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}. == Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}, known as deities of the sea and maritime safety: {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵島姫命|1=Ichikishimahime}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|多岐津姫命}}, and {{ill|Tagirihime-no-Mikoto|ja|多紀理姫命}}. Additional deities worshipped include {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto|ja|倉稲魂命}}, {{ill|Miyabime-no-Mikoto|ja|宮比命}}, {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|ja|猿田彦命}}, and {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|海津見大神}}. == Overview == According to legend, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyo|ja|允恭天皇}}, a courtier named {{ill|Uto Yasukata|ja|善知鳥安方}} was exiled to Sotogahama. Inspired by a divine dream of Takakura Myojin, he built a small shrine on the tidal flats and enshrined the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}. Upon Yasukata's death, an unfamiliar bird appeared near the shrine. The male bird called "Uto," while the female bird called "Yasukata." Locals revered these birds as Yasukata’s incarnations. However, when a hunter mistakenly shot the male bird, the fields were ravaged, and the hunter died mysteriously, believed to be a curse. The male bird was then respectfully buried. During the time of {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}}, the shrine had fallen into ruin but was rebuilt under his command during the {{ill|Daido era|ja|大同}}. The shrine was later patronized by the {{ill|Nanbu clan|ja|南部氏}} during their rule and continued to receive support from the {{ill|Tsugaru clan|ja|津軽氏}} after their rise in the Edo period. In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine was designated a {{ill|Gosha|ja|郷社}} in 1873 and elevated to a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} in 1876. In 1907, as a part of the [[Shrine Consolidation Policy]] it incorporated the local {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷神社}} in July and {{ill|Watatsumi Shrine|ja|海津見神社}} in September. In December 2020, the shrine began offering {{ill|ema|ja|絵馬}} in collaboration with the {{ill|Virtual YouTuber|ja|バーチャルYouTuber}} {{ill|Aomori Rinko|ja|青森りんこ}}.{{Cite web|title=Collaboration Ema Now Available: Uto Shrine × "Aomori Rinko"|url=https://www.navitabi.jp/article/3831|website=Navitabi North Tohoku|accessdate=2021-01-14}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Rhinoceros auklet|ja|ウトウ}} (Uto/sea bird) * {{ill|Utou|ja|善知鳥}} (Noh play) * {{ill|Shikō Munakata|ja|棟方志功}} – a printmaker and artist who spent his childhood near the shrine. == References == == External Links == {{Commonscat}} * [http://utojinja.sakura.ne.jp/ Official Website of Uto Shrine] * [http://www.aomori-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja/tousei/jinja/12.htm Uto Shrine - Aomori Prefecture Shrine Association] *
