{{Unreferenced|date=August 2020}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Uto Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Cerorhinca monocerata.jpg |location = {{ill|Aomori|en|Aomori|WD=Q146790|lt=Aomori, Aomori}}, Yasukata 2-7-18, Aomori Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|40.826694|N|140.742444|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=Three Munakata Goddesses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Sanjojin|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=}} |Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = Unknown |Main Hall = Shinmei-zukuri |alternate_name = |Main Festival = September 15 |Rituals = |Map = {{location map|Japan Aomori|lat_deg=40|lat_min=49|lat_sec=36.1|lon_deg=140|lon_min=44|lon_sec=32.8|label=Uto Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{Coord|40|49|36.1|N|140|44|32.8|E}}}} |location = {{ill|Yasukata|WD=Q30936056}} |deity = {{ill|Three Goddesses of Munakata|WD=Q10948069}} |architecture_style = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545}} }}
'''Uto Shrine''' (善知鳥神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Aomori|en|Aomori|WD=Q146790|lt=Aomori, Aomori}}, Yasukata, Aomori Prefecture. Its former shrine Rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.
== Deities ==
The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=Three Munakata Goddesses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Sanjojin|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=}}, known as deities of the sea and maritime safety: {{ill|Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵島姫命|1=Ichikishima-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|多岐津姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タギツヒメ|1=Tagitsuhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10932592}}, and {{ill|Tagirihime-no-Mikoto|ja|多紀理姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagirihime-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ|1=Takiribime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10932870}}. Additional deities worshipped include {{ill|Ukanomitama|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto|ja|倉稲魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukanomitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ}}, {{ill|Miyabime-no-Mikoto|ja|宮比命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyabime-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|ja|猿田彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}}, and {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|海津見大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watatsumi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ワタツミ|1=Watatsumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1474187}}.
== Overview ==
According to legend, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|en|Emperor Ingyō|WD=Q349258|lt=Emperor Ingyo}}, a courtier named {{ill|Uto Yasukata|ja|善知鳥安方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uto Yasukata|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was exiled to Sotogahama. Inspired by a divine dream of Takakura Myojin, he built a small shrine on the tidal flats and enshrined the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=Three Munakata Goddesses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Sanjojin|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=}}. Upon Yasukata's death, an unfamiliar bird appeared near the shrine. The male bird called "Uto," while the female bird called "Yasukata." Locals revered these birds as Yasukata’s incarnations. However, when a hunter mistakenly shot the male bird, the fields were ravaged, and the hunter died mysteriously, believed to be a curse. The male bird was then respectfully buried.
During the time of {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|en|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|WD=Q941979|lt=Sakanoue no Tamuramaro}}, the shrine had fallen into ruin but was rebuilt under his command during the {{ill|Datong (disambiguation)|en|Datong (disambiguation)|WD=Q295082|lt=Daido era}}. The shrine was later patronized by the {{ill|Nanbu clan|en|Nanbu clan|WD=Q2975330|lt=Nanbu clan}} during their rule and continued to receive support from the {{ill|Tsugaru clan|en|Tsugaru clan|WD=Q1062437|lt=Tsugaru clan}} after their rise in the Edo period. In the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period}}, the shrine was designated a {{ill|Gosha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gosha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} in 1873 and elevated to a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} in 1876. In 1907, as a part of the [[Shrine Consolidation Policy]] it incorporated the local {{ill|Inari shrine|en|Inari shrine|WD=Q514480|lt=Inari Shrine}} in July and {{ill|Watatsumi Shrine|ja|海津見神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watatsumi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in September.
In December 2020, the shrine began offering {{ill|Ema (Shinto)|en|Ema (Shinto)|WD=Q687288|lt=Ema (Shinto)}} in collaboration with the {{ill|VTuber|en|VTuber|WD=Q55155641|lt=Virtual YouTuber}} {{ill|Aomori Rinko|ja|青森りんこ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Rinko|1=Aomori Rinko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q104591587}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Collaboration Ema Now Available: Uto Shrine × "Aomori Rinko"|url=https://www.navitabi.jp/article/3831|website=Navitabi North Tohoku|accessdate=2021-01-14}}</ref>

'''Uto Shrine''' (善知鳥神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Aomori|en|Aomori|WD=Q146790|lt=Aomori, Aomori|1=Aomori}}, Yasukata, Aomori Prefecture. Its former shrine Rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.
== Deities ==
The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=Three Munakata Goddesses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Sanjojin|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=}}, known as deities of the sea and maritime safety: {{ill|Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵島姫命|1=Ichikishima-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|多岐津姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タギツヒメ|1=Tagitsuhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10932592}}, and {{ill|Tagirihime-no-Mikoto|ja|多紀理姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagirihime-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ|1=Tagorihime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10932870}}. Additional deities worshipped include {{ill|Ukanomitama|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto|ja|倉稲魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukanomitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ|1=Ukanomitama}}, {{ill|Miyabime-no-Mikoto|ja|宮比命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyabime-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|ja|猿田彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}}, and {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|海津見大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watatsumi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ワタツミ|1=Watatsumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1474187}}.
== Overview ==
According to legend, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|en|Emperor Ingyō|WD=Q349258|lt=Emperor Ingyo|1=Emperor Ingyō}}, a courtier named {{ill|Uto Yasukata|ja|善知鳥安方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uto Yasukata|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was exiled to Sotogahama. Inspired by a divine dream of Takakura Myojin, he built a small shrine on the tidal flats and enshrined the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=Three Munakata Goddesses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Sanjojin|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=}}. Upon Yasukata's death, an unfamiliar bird appeared near the shrine. The male bird called "Uto," while the female bird called "Yasukata." Locals revered these birds as Yasukata’s incarnations. However, when a hunter mistakenly shot the male bird, the fields were ravaged, and the hunter died mysteriously, believed to be a curse. The male bird was then respectfully buried.
During the time of {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|en|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|WD=Q941979|lt=Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|1=Sakanoue no Tamuramaro}}, the shrine had fallen into ruin but was rebuilt under his command during the {{ill|Datong (disambiguation)|en|Datong (disambiguation)|WD=Q295082|lt=Daido era|1=Datong (disambiguation)}}. The shrine was later patronized by the {{ill|Nanbu clan|en|Nanbu clan|WD=Q2975330|lt=Nanbu clan|1=Nanbu clan}} during their rule and continued to receive support from the {{ill|Tsugaru clan|en|Tsugaru clan|WD=Q1062437|lt=Tsugaru clan|1=Tsugaru clan}} after their rise in the Edo period. In the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era}}, the shrine was designated a {{ill|Gosha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gosha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} in 1873 and elevated to a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} in 1876. In 1907, as a part of the [[Shrine Consolidation Policy]] it incorporated the local {{ill|Inari shrine|en|Inari shrine|WD=Q514480|lt=Inari Shrine|1=Inari shrine}} in July and {{ill|Watatsumi Shrine|ja|海津見神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watatsumi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in September.
In December 2020, the shrine began offering {{ill|Ema (Shinto)|en|Ema (Shinto)|WD=Q687288|lt=Ema (Shinto)|1=Ema (Shinto)}} in collaboration with the {{ill|VTuber|en|VTuber|WD=Q55155641|lt=Virtual YouTuber|1=VTuber}} {{ill|Aomori Rinko|ja|青森りんこ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Rinko|1=Aomori Rinko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q104591587}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Collaboration Ema Now Available: Uto Shrine × "Aomori Rinko"|url=https://www.navitabi.jp/article/3831|website=Navitabi North Tohoku|accessdate=2021-01-14}}</ref>

{{Unreferenced|date=August 2020}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Uto Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Cerorhinca monocerata.jpg |location = {{ill|Aomori|en|Aomori|WD=Q146790|lt=Aomori, Aomori|1=Aomori}}, Yasukata 2-7-18, Aomori Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|40.826694|N|140.742444|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=Three Munakata Goddesses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Sanjojin|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=}} |Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = Unknown |Main Hall = Shinmei-zukuri |alternate_name = |Main Festival = September 15 |Rituals = |Map = {{location map|Japan Aomori|lat_deg=40|lat_min=49|lat_sec=36.1|lon_deg=140|lon_min=44|lon_sec=32.8|label=Uto Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{Coord|40|49|36.1|N|140|44|32.8|E}}}} |location = {{ill|Yasukata|WD=Q30936056|1=Yasukata}} |deity = {{ill|Three Goddesses of Munakata|WD=Q10948069|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}} |architecture_style = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} }}
'''Uto Shrine''' (善知鳥神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Aomori|en|Aomori|WD=Q146790|lt=Aomori, Aomori|1=Aomori}}, Yasukata, Aomori Prefecture. Its former shrine Rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.
== Deities ==
The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=Three Munakata Goddesses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Sanjojin|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=}}, known as deities of the sea and maritime safety: {{ill|Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵島姫命|1=Ichikishima-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|多岐津姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タギツヒメ|1=Tagitsuhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10932592}}, and {{ill|Tagirihime-no-Mikoto|ja|多紀理姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagirihime-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ|1=Tagorihime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10932870}}. Additional deities worshipped include {{ill|Ukanomitama|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto|ja|倉稲魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukanomitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ|1=Ukanomitama}}, {{ill|Miyabime-no-Mikoto|ja|宮比命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyabime-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|ja|猿田彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}}, and {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|海津見大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watatsumi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ワタツミ|1=Watatsumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1474187}}.
== Overview ==
According to legend, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|en|Emperor Ingyō|WD=Q349258|lt=Emperor Ingyo|1=Emperor Ingyō}}, a courtier named {{ill|Uto Yasukata|ja|善知鳥安方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uto Yasukata|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was exiled to Sotogahama. Inspired by a divine dream of Takakura Myojin, he built a small shrine on the tidal flats and enshrined the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=Three Munakata Goddesses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Sanjojin|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=}}. Upon Yasukata's death, an unfamiliar bird appeared near the shrine. The male bird called "Uto," while the female bird called "Yasukata." Locals revered these birds as Yasukata’s incarnations. However, when a hunter mistakenly shot the male bird, the fields were ravaged, and the hunter died mysteriously, believed to be a curse. The male bird was then respectfully buried.
During the time of {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|en|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|WD=Q941979|lt=Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|1=Sakanoue no Tamuramaro}}, the shrine had fallen into ruin but was rebuilt under his command during the {{ill|Datong (disambiguation)|en|Datong (disambiguation)|WD=Q295082|lt=Daido era|1=Datong (disambiguation)}}. The shrine was later patronized by the {{ill|Nanbu clan|en|Nanbu clan|WD=Q2975330|lt=Nanbu clan|1=Nanbu clan}} during their rule and continued to receive support from the {{ill|Tsugaru clan|en|Tsugaru clan|WD=Q1062437|lt=Tsugaru clan|1=Tsugaru clan}} after their rise in the Edo period. In the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era}}, the shrine was designated a {{ill|Gosha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gosha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} in 1873 and elevated to a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} in 1876. In 1907, as a part of the [[Shrine Consolidation Policy]] it incorporated the local {{ill|Inari shrine|en|Inari shrine|WD=Q514480|lt=Inari Shrine|1=Inari shrine}} in July and {{ill|Watatsumi Shrine|ja|海津見神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watatsumi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in September.
In December 2020, the shrine began offering {{ill|Ema (Shinto)|en|Ema (Shinto)|WD=Q687288|lt=Ema (Shinto)|1=Ema (Shinto)}} in collaboration with the {{ill|VTuber|en|VTuber|WD=Q55155641|lt=Virtual YouTuber|1=VTuber}} {{ill|Aomori Rinko|ja|青森りんこ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Rinko|1=Aomori Rinko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q104591587}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Collaboration Ema Now Available: Uto Shrine × "Aomori Rinko"|url=https://www.navitabi.jp/article/3831|website=Navitabi North Tohoku|accessdate=2021-01-14}}</ref>

The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=Three Munakata Goddesses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Sanjojin|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=}}, known as deities of the sea and maritime safety: {{ill|Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵島姫命|1=Ichikishima-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|多岐津姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タギツヒメ|1=Tagitsuhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10932592}}, and {{ill|Tagirihime-no-Mikoto|ja|多紀理姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagirihime-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ|1=Tagorihime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10932870}}. Additional deities worshipped include {{ill|Ukanomitama|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto|ja|倉稲魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukanomitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ|1=Ukanomitama}}, {{ill|Miyabime-no-Mikoto|ja|宮比命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyabime-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|ja|猿田彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}}, and {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|海津見大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watatsumi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ワタツミ|1=Watatsumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1474187}}.
== Overview ==
According to legend, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|en|Emperor Ingyō|WD=Q349258|lt=Emperor Ingyo|1=Emperor Ingyō}}, a courtier named {{ill|Uto Yasukata|ja|善知鳥安方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uto Yasukata|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was exiled to Sotogahama. Inspired by a divine dream of Takakura Myojin, he built a small shrine on the tidal flats and enshrined the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=Three Munakata Goddesses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Sanjojin|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=}}. Upon Yasukata's death, an unfamiliar bird appeared near the shrine. The male bird called "Uto," while the female bird called "Yasukata." Locals revered these birds as Yasukata’s incarnations. However, when a hunter mistakenly shot the male bird, the fields were ravaged, and the hunter died mysteriously, believed to be a curse. The male bird was then respectfully buried.
During the time of {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|en|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|WD=Q941979|lt=Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|1=Sakanoue no Tamuramaro}}, the shrine had fallen into ruin but was rebuilt under his command during the {{ill|Datong (disambiguation)|en|Datong (disambiguation)|WD=Q295082|lt=Daido era|1=Datong (disambiguation)}}. The shrine was later patronized by the {{ill|Nanbu clan|en|Nanbu clan|WD=Q2975330|lt=Nanbu clan|1=Nanbu clan}} during their rule and continued to receive support from the {{ill|Tsugaru clan|en|Tsugaru clan|WD=Q1062437|lt=Tsugaru clan|1=Tsugaru clan}} after their rise in the Edo period. In the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era}}, the shrine was designated a {{ill|Gosha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gosha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} in 1873 and elevated to a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} in 1876. In 1907, as a part of the [[Shrine Consolidation Policy]] it incorporated the local {{ill|Inari shrine|en|Inari shrine|WD=Q514480|lt=Inari Shrine|1=Inari shrine}} in July and {{ill|Watatsumi Shrine|ja|海津見神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watatsumi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in September.
In December 2020, the shrine began offering {{ill|Ema (Shinto)|en|Ema (Shinto)|WD=Q687288|lt=Ema (Shinto)|1=Ema (Shinto)}} in collaboration with the {{ill|VTuber|en|VTuber|WD=Q55155641|lt=Virtual YouTuber|1=VTuber}} {{ill|Aomori Rinko|ja|青森りんこ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Rinko|1=Aomori Rinko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q104591587}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Collaboration Ema Now Available: Uto Shrine × "Aomori Rinko"|url=https://www.navitabi.jp/article/3831|website=Navitabi North Tohoku|accessdate=2021-01-14}}</ref>
== Related Articles ==

'''Uto Shrine''' (善知鳥神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Aomori|en|Aomori|WD=Q146790|lt=Aomori, Aomori|1=Aomori}}, Yasukata, Aomori Prefecture. Its former shrine Rank was {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.
== Deities ==
The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=Three Munakata Goddesses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Sanjojin|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=}}, known as deities of the sea and maritime safety: {{ill|Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵島姫命|1=Ichikishima-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|多岐津姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タギツヒメ|1=Tagitsuhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10932592}}, and {{ill|Tagirihime-no-Mikoto|ja|多紀理姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagirihime-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ|1=Tagorihime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10932870}}. Additional deities worshipped include {{ill|Ukanomitama|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto|ja|倉稲魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukanomitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ|1=Ukanomitama}}, {{ill|Miyabime-no-Mikoto|ja|宮比命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyabime-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|ja|猿田彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}}, and {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|海津見大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watatsumi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ワタツミ|1=Watatsumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1474187}}.
== Overview ==
According to legend, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|en|Emperor Ingyō|WD=Q349258|lt=Emperor Ingyo|1=Emperor Ingyō}}, a courtier named {{ill|Uto Yasukata|ja|善知鳥安方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uto Yasukata|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was exiled to Sotogahama. Inspired by a divine dream of Takakura Myojin, he built a small shrine on the tidal flats and enshrined the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=Three Munakata Goddesses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Sanjojin|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=}}. Upon Yasukata's death, an unfamiliar bird appeared near the shrine. The male bird called "Uto," while the female bird called "Yasukata." Locals revered these birds as Yasukata’s incarnations. However, when a hunter mistakenly shot the male bird, the fields were ravaged, and the hunter died mysteriously, believed to be a curse. The male bird was then respectfully buried.
During the time of {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|en|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|WD=Q941979|lt=Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|1=Sakanoue no Tamuramaro}}, the shrine had fallen into ruin but was rebuilt under his command during the {{ill|Datong (disambiguation)|en|Datong (disambiguation)|WD=Q295082|lt=Daido era|1=Datong (disambiguation)}}. The shrine was later patronized by the {{ill|Nanbu clan|en|Nanbu clan|WD=Q2975330|lt=Nanbu clan|1=Nanbu clan}} during their rule and continued to receive support from the {{ill|Tsugaru clan|en|Tsugaru clan|WD=Q1062437|lt=Tsugaru clan|1=Tsugaru clan}} after their rise in the Edo period. In the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era}}, the shrine was designated a {{ill|Gosha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gosha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} in 1873 and elevated to a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} in 1876. In 1907, as a part of the [[Shrine Consolidation Policy]] it incorporated the local {{ill|Inari shrine|en|Inari shrine|WD=Q514480|lt=Inari Shrine|1=Inari shrine}} in July and {{ill|Watatsumi Shrine|ja|海津見神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watatsumi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in September.
In December 2020, the shrine began offering {{ill|Ema (Shinto)|en|Ema (Shinto)|WD=Q687288|lt=Ema (Shinto)|1=Ema (Shinto)}} in collaboration with the {{ill|VTuber|en|VTuber|WD=Q55155641|lt=Virtual YouTuber|1=VTuber}} {{ill|Aomori Rinko|ja|青森りんこ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Rinko|1=Aomori Rinko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q104591587}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Collaboration Ema Now Available: Uto Shrine × "Aomori Rinko"|url=https://www.navitabi.jp/article/3831|website=Navitabi North Tohoku|accessdate=2021-01-14}}</ref>

Upon Yasukata's death, an unfamiliar bird appeared near the shrine. The male bird called "Uto," while the female bird called "Yasukata." Locals revered these birds as Yasukata’s incarnations. However, when a hunter mistakenly shot the male bird, the fields were ravaged, and the hunter died mysteriously, believed to be a curse. The male bird was then respectfully buried.
During the time of {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|en|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|WD=Q941979|lt=Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|1=Sakanoue no Tamuramaro}}, the shrine had fallen into ruin but was rebuilt under his command during the {{ill|Datong (disambiguation)|en|Datong (disambiguation)|WD=Q295082|lt=Daido era|1=Datong (disambiguation)}}. The shrine was later patronized by the {{ill|Nanbu clan|en|Nanbu clan|WD=Q2975330|lt=Nanbu clan|1=Nanbu clan}} during their rule and continued to receive support from the {{ill|Tsugaru clan|en|Tsugaru clan|WD=Q1062437|lt=Tsugaru clan|1=Tsugaru clan}} after their rise in the Edo period. In the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era}}, the shrine was designated a {{ill|Gosha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gosha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} in 1873 and elevated to a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} in 1876. In 1907, as a part of the [[Shrine Consolidation Policy]] it incorporated the local {{ill|Inari shrine|en|Inari shrine|WD=Q514480|lt=Inari Shrine|1=Inari shrine}} in July and {{ill|Watatsumi Shrine|ja|海津見神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watatsumi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in September.
In December 2020, the shrine began offering {{ill|Ema (Shinto)|en|Ema (Shinto)|WD=Q687288|lt=Ema (Shinto)|1=Ema (Shinto)}} in collaboration with the {{ill|VTuber|en|VTuber|WD=Q55155641|lt=Virtual YouTuber|1=VTuber}} {{ill|Aomori Rinko|ja|青森りんこ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Rinko|1=Aomori Rinko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q104591587}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Collaboration Ema Now Available: Uto Shrine × "Aomori Rinko"|url=https://www.navitabi.jp/article/3831|website=Navitabi North Tohoku|accessdate=2021-01-14}}</ref>
